schools.summary: Gill: it is repeated over and over to impress it on their minds and show its importance and necessity.
Henry: the charge the same as before — to keep and do all God's commandments.

themes: All the commandments which I command thee this day shall ye observe to do, that ye may live
the duty pressed again and again
the way of life lying in obedience

schools.summary: Gill: this was the fortieth year, God going before them in the pillar of cloud and fire; the leading was to humble and prove them, to know what was in their heart.

themes: thou shalt remember all the way which the LORD... led thee these forty years... to humble thee, and to prove thee
the wilderness God's school for the heart
affliction a proving of faith (James 1:3)

schools.summary: Gill: he humbled and afflicted them with want of bread.
Keil & Delitzsch: fed with manna, that he might make them know man lives not by bread only but by every word from the LORD's mouth.

themes: man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the LORD
life depending on God's word, not the food
Christ's answer to Satan (Matt. 4:4)

schools.summary: Gill: they wanted not clothes all the forty years; some account for it by the clothes of those who died and the spoils of Amalek, others by the much clothing brought out of Egypt.

themes: Thy raiment waxed not old upon thee, neither did thy foot swell, these forty years
the constant providence of God over the least things
every need of his people watched

schools.summary: Gill: they were frequently to weigh in their hearts this matter, for their peace, comfort, and the glory of God.
Keil & Delitzsch: as a man chastens his son, so the LORD chastens.

themes: as a man chasteneth his son, so the LORD thy God chasteneth thee
the trials a father's discipline
whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth (Heb. 12:6)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to keep his commands not only because they are his, but because he had dealt so bountifully with them, providing food and raiment and afflicting them in love, and to walk in his ways and fear him.

themes: thou shalt keep the commandments of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, and to fear him
gratitude the spring of obedience
the bounty of God claiming his people's service

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan abounded with good things, a land flowing with milk and honey, with plenty for convenience and delight — another reason to serve the Lord.
Keil & Delitzsch: the good land of brooks and fountains.

themes: the LORD thy God bringeth thee into a good land, a land of brooks of water
the richness of the inheritance
the good gift binding them to the Giver

schools.summary: Gill: there were two harvests, barley at the passover and wheat at Pentecost, with great plenty; the land yielded vines, figs, pomegranates, oil, and honey.

themes: A land of wheat, and barley, and vines, and fig trees, and pomegranates; a land of oil olive, and honey
the rich fruitfulness of the land
God's lavish provision, a witness to his bounty

schools.summary: Gill: they should have plenty of all sorts of provisions, bread often put for all.
Clarke: the stones impregnated with iron, and brass dug from the hills — the most useful of metals.

themes: A land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness... a land whose stones are iron
the land furnished with every need
the completeness of God's gift, lacking nothing

schools.summary: Gill: as God would furnish them with plenty, they might eat liberally, not to intemperance; this shows we are to return thanks for a plentiful meal as well as ask a blessing on it.

themes: When thou hast eaten and art full, then thou shalt bless the LORD thy God
the grace of thankfulness amid plenty
every good gift acknowledged to the Giver (1 Tim. 4:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD is the Father of mercies and fountain of goodness; plenty is apt to induce forgetfulness of God, when it should rather keep him in remembrance.
Keil & Delitzsch: the warning to beware lest they forget.

themes: Beware that thou forget not the LORD thy God, in not keeping his commandments
the subtle peril of plenty
forgetting shown in disobedience

schools.summary: Gill: the danger of being continually full, day after day, indulged with great plenty.
Ellicott: recalls Jonadab's charge to the Rechabites to dwell in tents, that they might live long as strangers.

themes: when thou hast eaten and art full, and hast built goodly houses, and dwelt therein
the comforts that breed forgetfulness
the soul watchful when most at ease

schools.summary: Gill: the herds and flocks multiplying, having good pasture in so fruitful a land; all they had would be multiplied.

themes: when thy herds and thy flocks multiply, and thy silver and thy gold is multiplied
the rising tide of wealth trying the heart
set not your heart on increasing riches (Ps. 62:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the heart is apt to be lifted up when riches increase.
Wesley: the pride of receiving these things as if by one's own wisdom, valour, and industry, or for one's own merit.

themes: Then thine heart be lifted up, and thou forget the LORD thy God, which brought thee forth out of... Egypt
pride the root of forgetting God
filled, and the heart exalted, forgetting him (Hos. 13:6)

schools.summary: Gill: the wilderness of Paran, great and terrible, with fiery serpents and scorpions, and the water brought from the rock. Calvin: the water brought from the flinty rock when the people suffered thirst.

themes: Who led thee through that great and terrible wilderness... who brought thee forth water out of the rock of flint
God's care where nature offered death
that Rock was Christ (1 Cor. 10:4)

schools.summary: Gill: he fed them with manna all the forty years, which their fathers knew not.
Clarke: refers to the miracle of the manna (Exod. 16:13).

themes: Who fed thee... with manna... that he might humble thee, and... prove thee, to do thee good at thy latter end
the discipline aimed at a blessed issue
affliction working the peaceable fruit (Heb. 12:11)

schools.summary: Gill: these words connect with the warning, the secret boast of the heart, ascribing their prosperity to their own strength and labour.

themes: thou say in thine heart, My power and the might of mine hand hath gotten me this wealth
the lie of self-sufficiency forgetting God
what hast thou that thou didst not receive (1 Cor. 4:7)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to remember the LORD, the author of their beings and the God of their mercies, who had given them the fruitful land.
Clarke: God gives the power to get wealth.

themes: thou shalt remember the LORD thy God: for it is he that giveth thee power to get wealth
even our abilities God's gift
all of grace, to establish his covenant (James 1:17)

schools.summary: Gill: forgetting God is either not acknowledging his mercies but ascribing them to oneself, or forsaking his worship and serving other gods; Moses testifies they shall surely perish.

themes: if thou do at all forget the LORD... and walk after other gods... ye shall surely perish
apostasy ending in ruin
the strongest motive to faithfulness

schools.summary: Gill: they would be cut off or cast out as the nations were, the same sins, particularly idolatry, being committed; because they would not obey the voice of the LORD.

themes: As the nations which the LORD destroyeth before your face, so shall ye perish... ye would not be obedient
election no shield from judgment
God no respecter of persons (Rom. 2:11)
